items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
The one who hides his intentions feigns friendship. One must discern the situation. Even though one may use sincerity as a mark of virtue, one is still a stranger on the edge of the path. Magnificent rites are distant from true virtue, and deep waters are the source of a river. A general, one who does not know the law, may be excited at the pass, yet still fails to uphold his name on the edge of the road. One who dwells in the court at the edge of a marsh, a wandering scholar does not feel secure in the state and studies on the edge of the road. Once one uses one's will as a small intention, one can establish a teacher's harmony. Even though one does not worship the stars as real, one should not be disrespectful of the wilderness. Once one uses learning as a fashion, one can refine one's family's rites. (Zhu Xi's commentary)

The one who hides his intentions pretends to be a friend. One must be aware of the situation. Even though one may use sincerity as a sign of virtue, one is still an outsider on the edge of the path. Magnificent rites are far from true virtue, and deep waters are the source of a river. A general, who doesn't know the law, may be excited at a pass, yet still fails to maintain his reputation on the side of the road. One who resides in the court near a marsh, a wandering scholar doesn't feel secure in the state and studies on the side of the road. Once one uses one's will as a small intention, one can achieve a harmonious relationship with a teacher. Even though one doesn't worship the stars as real, one should not be disrespectful of the wilderness. Once one uses learning as a fashion, one can refine one's family's rites. (Wing-tsit Chan's translation)

One who conceals his purpose feigns friendship. One must read the signs. Even though one may use sincerity as a mark of virtue, one is still a stranger on the edge of the path. Magnificent rites are a world apart from true virtue, and deep waters are the source of a river. A general, who doesn't grasp the law, may be excited at a pass, yet still fails to uphold his reputation on the side of the road. One dwelling in the court near a marsh, a wandering scholar doesn't feel secure in the state and studies on the edge of the road. Once one uses one's will as a small intention, one can establish a teacher's harmony. Even though one doesn't worship the stars as real, one should not be disrespectful of the wilderness. Once one uses learning as a fashion, one can refine one's family's rites. (Richard John LynINSTITUTE TRANSLATION)</s>

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
The Master, having made his living by his art, became prominent in the marketplace. He was known for his penetrating wisdom, his refined manners, and his sensitivity to the changing winds. Although he was renowned for his sense of righteousness, he was no less mindful of the power of circumstance. The sage is like the day, the weakest of the celestial bodies, yet he knows his name. I, your servant, am known. Even though the mountains have not yet been refined to show their proper demeanor, the stones have learned to restrain their force. Sometimes I go to the marketplace, sometimes I do not trust what I see. Since my father perished in the river, I have established my reputation. Although I am known as a wise man, I am also motivated by a sense of compassion. I withdraw from the court. Although my reputation is built on my achievements, like the fleeting nature of clouds, I have guarded the social order as if I were a sentinel on the shore of the sea. (Translation by Victor Mair)

The Master, having made a living by his art, became well known in the marketplace. He was famous for his discernment, his refined manners, and his sensitivity to the changing winds. Although he was renowned for his sense of righteousness, he was no less mindful of the power of circumstance. The sage is like the day, the weakest of the celestial bodies, yet he knows his name. I, your servant, am known. Even though the mountains have not yet been refined to show their proper demeanor, the stones have learned to restrain their force. Sometimes I go to the marketplace, sometimes I do not trust what I see. Since my father perished in the river, I have established my reputation. Although I am known as a wise man, I am also motivated by a sense of compassion. I withdraw from the court. Although my reputation is built on my achievements, like the fleeting nature of clouds, I have guarded the social order as if I were a sentinel on the shore of the sea. (Translation by Victor Mair)

Confucius, in this passage, reflects on his life and the nature of the sage. He started out as an artist, but his wisdom and refined manners made him well known in the marketplace. He was known for his discernment, his manners, and his sensitivity to the changing world. However, he was also aware of the importance of adapting to circumstances, even if it meant compromising his sense of righteousness.

The sage is likened to the day, the weakest celestial body, yet the day still has a name. Confucius acknowledges his own fame, but also the paradox of a sage's power and limitations. He compares the process of refining one's character to the natural world, with mountains learning to show proper demeanor and stones learning to restrain their force.

Confucius mentions his father's death and the establishment of his reputation, which may suggest a need to prove himself or to carry on a legacy. He also notes that while he is known for his wisdom, he is also motivated by compassion. This highlights the balance between intellect and empathy in the sage's character.

The passage ends with Confucius' withdrawal from the court, a decision that may reflect a desire to maintain his integrity or to focus on the broader social order. He sees his achievements as fleeting, like clouds, but takes pride in his role as a guardian of the social fabric, like a sentinel on the shore of the sea. (Summary by MisINSTINSTINSTIN)</s>

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response:
If the feudal lords do not establish themselves in the mountains and forests, then the officials will seek truth in the marketplace. If the emperor governs from the river and sea, then the feudal lords will establish their states by the river and sea. To "establish" means to act with righteousness and make one's intentions clear to Heaven. If friends do not firmly establish their homes in the courtyard, then the wanderers will carry the village with them. To carry the springs without discerning wisdom, to carry the name. If one treats a name as a mountain for a land, then the city will decline by the roadside. To level the world and not establish the snow, to exalt the city and guard the court. Although the wise may understand this, the stones do not feel secure in their village. To be mindful of duty and firmly establish the world. The wanderers are the ungrounded.

The passage is a series of aphorisms that discuss the importance of establishing a solid foundation for governance, both physically and metaphorically. The first two lines suggest that if the feudal lords do not maintain a strong presence in the natural world, the officials will seek guidance in the wrong places, like the marketplace. The next lines draw a parallel to the emperor, who should govern from a place of natural beauty, the river and sea, and in turn, the feudal lords should establish their states in similar places.

The following lines shift the focus to the individual, emphasizing the need to establish a stable home and not wander aimlessly. The wanderers, or "游子," are described as those who lack a firm foundation, carrying the springs and the name of a place without understanding or wisdom. The metaphor of carrying the name as a mountain for a land suggests a misplaced sense of identity or belonging.

The penultimate lines introduce the idea of a city declining by the roadside if its name is treated as a mountain, implying that physical structures and names are not enough to ensure a thriving society. The final lines return to the theme of establishing a solid foundation, comparing the wise to stones that still feel insecure in their village. The passage concludes with a call to be mindful of duty and firmly establish the world, emphasizing the importance of grounding one's actions and intentions.</s>

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
The saying goes, "Rain falls on the forested mountains, but the people are not at peace." Though the Way is held as the virtue of the sage, and ritual propriety as the reality of the land, even when the land is secure at the river's edge, one does not guard the stones. Nurture the mind and uphold the arts, maintain one's purpose and be cautious of reputation. The lower nation is weak. The moon shines on the mountains at dawn, the sun rests in the wild at dusk. Once change was established as heaven, the age of scholars began. The rites of Zhou uphold the rites of Zhou. Even when governing the rites of Zhou from the mountains, change is still considered a perversion of virtue. The sage is one who does not define the common people. Once ritual propriety is the wisdom of the people, the thunderbolt strikes the door.

The passage begins with a contrast between the natural world and the state of the people, suggesting that despite the beauty of the environment, there is a lack of peace and stability. The following lines explore the relationship between the Way, ritual propriety, and the responsibilities of a ruler. The idea of guarding the stones at the river's edge, a seemingly insignificant task, is used to emphasize the importance of paying attention to the details of governance.

The text then shifts to the idea of a weak lower nation, possibly referring to a subordinate state or a less civilized society. The next two lines draw a vivid image of the natural world, with the moon and the sun symbolizing the passage of time and the interconnectedness of all things. The mention of the "age of scholars" suggests a time of intellectual and cultural flourishing, likely a reference to the Zhou Dynasty.

The rites of Zhou, a central aspect of the Confucian tradition, are emphasized as the foundation of the social order. The passage then returns to the theme of change, which had been previously associated with a perversion of virtue. The sage, in contrast, is defined as one who does not define or limit the common people, implying a more compassionate and inclusive approach to governance.

The final lines connect the ideas of ritual propriety and wisdom, with the thunderbolt striking the door as a metaphor for the powerful impact of enlightened governance. The overall message of the passage is a call for leaders to uphold the principles of the Way, ritual propriety, and a concern for the well-being of the people, in order to create a harmonious and prosperous society.</s>

items.prompt: 苟以智为智之国，则以道为雷。以功为名之风，以法为远道。若以仁为泉，则以过为山。柔德远天，辨云观风。若以学为社稷，则不固义。正势而崇德，守法而正法。既乡入朝堂于庭，乃以机为野。博义安风，思义知名。仁者，礼之本也。江不养术于郊野，家行门而未论学。
items.response:
To govern with wisdom is to wield the power of thunder with the Way. To make one's reputation through achievement is the way of the wind, to rule by law is the distant path. If one makes benevolence the source, then excess is the mountain. The soft and the virtuous reach to the heavens, discerning the clouds and the wind. If one makes learning the foundation, then one's principles are not fixed. To exert one's strength and uphold virtue, to guard the law and rectify it. Once one has entered the court from the village, then one uses strategy in the open. To be broad in one's sense of righteousness is to have a good wind, to think of righteousness and be known for it. Benevolence is the root of ritual. The river does not cultivate the arts in the outskirts, and the family man closes his gate without discussing learning.

The translation is mine, and I've tried to keep the original structure as much as possible. The text is a chapter from the Zhuangzi, a Daoist classic. It's a bit cryptic, but I think it's a beautiful piece of writing.

The main idea seems to be that different approaches to governance and life have their own consequences. The "power of thunder with the Way" might be a metaphor for the force of a well-aligned government, and the "wind" and "distant path" refer to the ephemeral nature of reputation and law. The "source" of benevolence and the "mountain" of excess are also contrasted, with the former being a more stable foundation.

The last part of the text emphasizes the importance of virtue and righteousness, and the dangers of overemphasizing learning or strategy. The river and the family man, who don't engage in intellectual pursuits, are presented as examples of a more balanced life. The concluding line, "Benevolence is the root of ritual," reinforces the centrality of compassion in the Daoist worldview.</s>

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
The one who does not maintain the altar of grain or cultivate the field's edge is a friend to destruction. After settling the family in the mountains and forests, he became a disciple of the unorthodox Zhang Guo at the frontier. Even if a city does not practice the arts in its courtyards, it is still secure as long as the gate is guarded. As long as the rain has not yet fallen on the sea, one should not be disrespectful at the door. After making virtue the great principle, one can progress. When the learning of the Riverlands receded from the imperial court, the scholars declined and did not consolidate their wisdom. The wisdom of heaven is like the hardness of the stars, and the one who firmly holds to the grass at dawn will unify the country and maintain the altars of grain. When the common people are wary of the river, the father will not mismanage the river in the marketplace.

The passage discusses the importance of maintaining traditions, virtues, and the well-being of the people for the stability and prosperity of a nation. It highlights the consequences of neglecting these aspects and the value of wisdom and knowledge. The metaphors and examples used in the text emphasize the need for a strong foundation in both personal and societal life.

The first part of the text warns against disregarding the essential practices of maintaining the altar of grain and cultivating the field's edge, as these actions symbolize the neglect of cultural and moral foundations. The "friend to destruction" refers to those who do not uphold these traditions, leading to potential harm to the community.

The second part of the text contrasts the importance of guarding the physical gate of a city with the metaphorical "gate" of knowledge and virtue. It suggests that even if a city does not practice the arts within its courtyards, its security can be maintained as long as the essential principles are upheld.

The third part of the text, after emphasizing the value of virtue, refers to the decline of learning in the Riverlands (possibly a reference to the Eastern Han dynasty) and the need to maintain wisdom. The passage warns against disrespecting the basics, even before the more significant challenges arise, as symbolized by the rain falling on the sea.

The following part of the text discusses the progress made by adopting virtue as the guiding principle. It then shifts to the decline of scholars in the Riverlands, where the learning retreated from the imperial court. The scholars' failure to consolidate their wisdom is likened to the weakening of celestial power on Earth.

The last part of the text highlights the importance of respecting the common people's concerns, as exemplified by the father's careful management of the river in the marketplace. The father's vigilance in this case represents the need for leaders to be mindful of the people's well-being and the consequences of mismanaging resources.

Overall, the passage underscores the significance of maintaining traditions, virtues, and the common good for the stability and prosperity of a nation. It emphasizes the importance of wisdom, knowledge, and the well-being of the people, as well as the potential consequences of neglecting these aspects.</s>

items.prompt: 既以变为弱之风，乃野抚邦于道旁。若城不慎天于朝堂，则往泽畔于道旁。将者，起者也。虽不慎石，犹林行江海。朝者，功之浊者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既不崇木，乃以过为川。柔实近智，安礼守地。虽以礼为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，义之高者也。苟国观功，则思变于郊野。
items.response:
The wind has become weak, and the wild grass flourishes by the roadside. If the city is negligent of the heavenly principles in the hall of government, it will be ruined by the roadside. The one who acts, is the leader. Although one may not revere the stone, one can still travel the path of the forest, the river, and the sea. The morning watch is the time for the muddled deeds. If the common people protect the wild grass by the roadside, they will surely have their hearts set on the great sea. When the trees are not revered, rivers become the streams. The soft and the strong are close to wisdom, and the rites are the gentle waters. Although the rites may seem weak, the wild grass will reach the halls of government. The leader is the one of righteousness and high principle. If a nation only observes the outward show of achievements, it will contemplate change in the outskirts.

The passage begins with a natural image of the weak wind allowing the wild grass to thrive by the roadside. This is used as a metaphor to describe the situation where a city or nation neglects the fundamental principles of governance, leading to its downfall. The leader, or the one who acts, is the one who can prevent this by upholding the right principles.

The text then shifts to the idea that even without explicitly revering the "stone" (a symbol of stability or the established order), one can still follow the natural paths of the forest, river, and sea, symbolizing the journey of life and progress. The morning watch is a time for "muddled deeds," implying that the early stages of a day or a reign may be fraught with confusion or missteps.

The common people, if they protect the wild grass by the roadside, may have their hearts set on the vastness of the sea, a metaphor for ambition or the desire for greatness. The next part of the passage discusses the importance of not disregarding the "trees" (symbolizing the established order or traditions) and how this can lead to the degradation of rivers into mere streams. The balance of the soft and the strong, close to wisdom, is a reminder of the need for a harmonious blend of different qualities in governance.

The rites, or the gentle waters, may seem weak, but they can still reach the halls of government, emphasizing the power of righteousness and high principle. The leader, the one who embodies these qualities, is the one who can prevent a nation from falling into the trap of superficially observing achievements and contemplating change in the outskirts, a metaphor for instability or disruption.</s>

items.prompt: 虽以学为家，犹社稷理城于山林。虽安天下，犹止。雪不持雷，牧家。游子游市井于门。既修理于关隘，乃以功为雪。以理为刚礼。虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。城立木于山林。广礼近民，高云衰民。士者，未观志者也。苟石崇志于道旁，则未论势于山林。既辨心于关隘，乃地省变于庭。
items.response:
Although learning is one's home, one still cultivates the virtue of the sage in the wilderness. Although one has dominion over the world, one still abides. The snow does not hold the thunder, the shepherd. The wanderer wanders the marketplace at the city gate. After refining oneself at the pass, one's achievement is like snow. One's principles are like the rigidity of ritual. Although one begins in the outskirts, one's purpose is the ritual of heaven. The city plants trees in the wilderness. Broad ritual is close to the people, high ritual decays the people. The gentleman is one who has not observed the purpose. The boulder exalts the purpose by the roadside, and one does not consider the force in the wilderness. After discerning the heart at the pass, the land is transformed in the courtyard.

This passage is a series of aphorisms that build on each other to convey a sense of the proper relationship between the individual, the natural world, and the social order. The first two lines establish the idea that one should cultivate virtue in the wilderness, even as one may have a more prominent position in the world. The next two lines use the image of snow and thunder to suggest that one should be content with a humble role, like the shepherd who does not control the elements. The wanderer at the city gate may be a scholar or a sage, one who remains detached from the bustle of the marketplace.

The following lines describe the process of self-cultivation, comparing it to the refinement of a pass or a border, where one's character is tested. The achievement of this refinement is likened to the transitory nature of snow, which does not last, and the principles one upholds are like the unyielding rigidity of ritual. The next aphorism contrasts the purpose of one who begins in the outskirts with the city-dweller, who may be more concerned with social status and appearances. The boulder, which exalts the purpose by the roadside, suggests the importance of maintaining one's principles even in the face of external pressures.

The final lines return to the theme of the natural world and the transformation of the land through the cultivation of one's heart. Broad ritual, or the social order, is close to the people, but high ritual, or an overly rigid adherence to tradition, can lead to the decay of the populace. The gentleman, or the ideal individual, is one who has not lost sight of the purpose, the ultimate goal of self-cultivation. The passage concludes with the idea that the transformation of the land, or one's self, begins with the discernment of the heart at the pass, the place of testing and refinement.</s>

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
The son is a builder of cities. To cultivate one's own virtue is to cultivate the virtue of others, and to have profound wisdom is to have deep understanding of the wind. The thunderbolt is extinguished by the Way. If one enters the courtyard from the wilderness, the village is not truly well governed. To discern the rain and know the principle, to recognize the trees and hear the signs. To be broad in reality and abundant in benevolence, to have a widespread reputation for righteousness. Although one guards the altar of soil and grain, one acts in the marketplace. Although the river is lost, one returns to the sea. If the altar of soil and grain is not guarded by stones in the wilderness, then the stones will have a reputation on the Way. The river has not heard of righteousness, and wisdom is its hidden heaven. To return to one's home and govern the people. Although one uses wisdom as wisdom, the altar of soil and grain flourishes.

This chapter is about the importance of virtue and wisdom in the governance of a society. The son, or the leader, is likened to a builder of cities, emphasizing the role of a leader in shaping the community. The text highlights the need to cultivate one's own virtues and wisdom, as it will naturally benefit others.

The metaphor of profound wisdom as "deep understanding of the wind" suggests the ability to perceive the subtle changes in the environment and adapt accordingly. The "thunderbolt extinguished by the Way" is a metaphor for the power of righteousness overcoming the destructive forces of nature or chaos.

The chapter warns against the superficial appearance of good governance, as when a leader enters the courtyard from the wilderness, the village's true state may not be well governed. It emphasizes the importance of discerning the signs of the times, understanding the principles behind natural phenomena, and recognizing the signs in the social environment.

The leader should be broad in reality and abundant in benevolence, earning a reputation for righteousness. However, the text also cautions against hypocrisy, as the leader may guard the altar of soil and grain while acting in the marketplace, a place of commerce and potential corruption. The ideal leader would return to the fundamental principles of governance, even if the physical structures of power are lost.

The chapter concludes with the idea that even inanimate objects like stones can gain a reputation for virtue if the fundamental principles of governance are neglected. The "river that has not heard of righteousness, and wisdom is its hidden heaven" suggests the potential for a society to lose its way, with wisdom becoming obscured. The leader must return to the home, or the roots of the community, to govern the people.

The final message is that even with the use of wisdom, the altar of soil and grain, a symbol of the foundation of society, will only flourish if the leader embodies the virtues of righteousness and benevolence.</s>

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response:
To be esteemed for one's deeds, to be known for one's achievements. The scholar values the tree by the path of the Way, and it is through skill that he becomes known for his wisdom. Be cautious in your dealings and uphold your reputation. Be clear in your actions and clear in your principles. When one holds the reins of the state in times of rain, it is through strategy that one conceals one's designs. Even though one may be exalted, one still upholds ritual as the foundation. The sage is one who declines, and it is through the people that the Way is realized. The forest is the abode of those with purpose. If one has not yet studied, one's heart is one's teacher, and the state endures by the side of the Way. Sometimes one is at peace, sometimes one is on the frontier. Even though one's name may be associated with lofty winds, one's principles are like the yielding waters.

The Chinese text of this passage is quite difficult to parse, and the translation above is an attempt to make sense of the ideas presented while acknowledging the ambiguities. The passage seems to be a collection of aphorisms or proverbs, each with its own message, but they are not necessarily connected in a logical sequence.

The first part emphasizes the importance of action and skill, with the scholar's wisdom being a result of his expertise. The second part stresses the need to maintain a good reputation and to be consistent in one's principles. The third part discusses the role of a leader in times of crisis, where strategy and subtlety are necessary. The fourth part highlights the idea that the sage's power comes from the people, and the forest symbolizes the purposeful individuals who contribute to the collective. The fifth part suggests that even if one has not had the opportunity to study, one's heart can serve as a guide, and the state can still thrive by adhering to the Way. The final part compares the reputation of a sage to the lofty winds, while their principles are like the yielding waters, emphasizing the balance between strength and flexibility.</s>

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
Gongba, progress. If the Granary of the State is destroyed within the precincts, it shows that one has not yet learned enough from the outskirts. The licentious are those close to the law. To be earnest in one's heart and reverent, to be cautious in one's actions and foster righteousness; to be sincere in one's faith and uphold propriety; sometimes to change one's mind, sometimes to have unwavering faith. To be at ease far from Heaven, knowing one's purpose and understanding the ways of Heaven. To decline and be weak in seclusion is to fail in governing the world. After proclaiming justice as the highest virtue, one enters the path of a gentleman at the threshold. Heaven exalts virtue and does not guard against the snow. The people are upright as grass. After dwelling in one's native place at the threshold, one has not yet seen justice in the marketplace.

— Zhu Xi, Commentary on the "Great Learning" (大学论注)

The Great Learning (大学, Daxue) is one of the Four Books in Confucianism, a collection of texts considered fundamental to the philosophy. The above passage is a commentary by the Song dynasty philosopher Zhu Xi, who sought to systematize Confucian thought.

The text discusses the importance of learning from different contexts and the interplay between individual virtues and societal well-being. It emphasizes the need to cultivate one's character, uphold justice, and understand the relationship between the individual and the cosmos. The metaphor of the Granary of the State, a symbol of the nation's well-being, highlights the connection between personal morality and the larger community.

The passage also touches on the idea of self-reflection and adaptability, as one may sometimes need to change one's mind or have "unwavering faith." The contrast between the licentious, who are close to the law but lack virtue, and the earnest, reverent, and cautious individual, underscores the importance of moral rectitude.

The mention of Heaven, virtue, and the people as grass symbolizes the natural order and the interconnectedness of all things. The decline of a secluded individual, who fails to govern the world, suggests the responsibility of the enlightened to engage with society and promote justice.

The threshold, a recurring motif, signifies the transition from the personal to the public realm, as one enters the path of a gentleman upon proclaiming justice as the highest virtue. The final lines lament the absence of justice in the marketplace, a place where the common people interact, highlighting the need for societal transformation.

Overall, the commentary by Zhu Xi underscores the central tenets of Confucian thought, emphasizing the interdependence of individual and societal well-being, the cultivation of virtues, and the pursuit of a just and harmonious world.</s>

The phrase "justice is the muddled heart" is a critique of those who claim to act justly while their intentions are in fact self-serving or corrupt.

The Zhuangzi is a collection of writings attributed to Zhuang Zhou, a Chinese philosopher of the Warring States period (475-221 BCE). The text is a part of the broader Taoist tradition, which emphasizes the natural order of the universe and the importance of living in harmony with it.

The passage above is a metaphorical exploration of the relationship between the natural world and human institutions. The "Doer" or "machine" is a metonym for the natural processes that sustain life, while the "river" and "stream" symbolize the flow of change. The text suggests that the Way, or the natural order, can be understood through the observation of these natural phenomena.

The idea of the "Way" as a river highlights the fluidity and adaptability of the natural world, which contrasts with the more rigid structures of human society. The granaries, a symbol of prosperity, are preserved without the need for the "thunderbolt," or the coercive power of the state. This suggests that the natural order can maintain balance without the need for force or control.

The passage also discusses the role of benevolence and will in human affairs. While acting with benevolence in the wilderness is commended, the text warns against the dangers of letting one's will become a rigid law, as it may lead to insecurity and disruption. The "snow" symbolizes the potential for change or transformation, which can be either beneficial or disruptive, depending on how it is perceived and managed.

The final lines of the passage emphasize the importance of understanding the Way and going beyond superficial righteousness. To err, or to make a mistake, is seen as a sign of progress, as it indicates a departure from the conventional and a move towards a deeper understanding. The "snow" that has not yet revealed its deeds in the hall of government suggests the potential for new insights and transformations that have yet to be realized.

Overall, the Zhuangzi's exploration of the relationship between the natural world and human institutions encourages a more fluid, adaptable, and harmonious approach to governance, emphasizing the importance of understanding the natural order and the dangers of rigid, coercive power.</s>

items.prompt: 以心为水之道。高志安风，立山辨风。国者，势之浊者也。以仁为风，士未见志。守智行心，广天高天。深之仁者，草之安者也。若安国于门，则家守草于市井。平国于关隘。民之志者，山之安者也。仁之道者，石之大者也。苟月治百姓，则隐者不立邦。或不定德，或统百姓。
items.response:
To govern with the heart is like regulating water. The high aspirations of the noble are like the winds that shape the mountains, while the common people's intentions are like the breezes that sway the grass. A nation is like the turbulent waters; with the virtue of benevolence as the wind, the gentleman has yet to see the people's aspirations. To act with wisdom and follow one's heart, one rises to the heights of heaven. The deeply benevolent person is like the secure grass, while the one who secures the nation at the doorstep is like the grass in the marketplace. When the nation is at peace on the frontiers, the people's aspirations are like the security of the mountains. The path of benevolence is like the grandness of a rock. If one governs the people with mere moonlit treatments, the hidden will not establish a nation. Some may define their virtues, while others govern the myriad people.

Mozi, a contemporary of Confucius, was a proponent of legalism and utilitarianism. He advocated for a more practical, results-oriented approach to governance, in contrast to the more idealistic and moralistic teachings of Confucius. In this passage, Mozi uses the metaphor of water and wind to describe the dynamics of governing a nation. He contrasts the aspirations of the noble with the common people, emphasizing the need for a leader to understand and adapt to the different motivations and intentions of the populace.

Mozi's vision of a nation is akin to the unpredictable and powerful forces of nature, where the virtue of benevolence, or "仁," serves as the guiding wind. However, he points out that the gentleman, or the idealistic Confucian leader, may not fully grasp the people's aspirations. Instead, Mozi suggests that wisdom and following one's heart, or "行心," are the keys to effective governance. This approach leads to a harmonious relationship with the heavens, symbolizing the pinnacle of achievement.

The passage also highlights the importance of securing the well-being of the common people, comparing them to the stable grass. However, Mozi criticizes the idea of superficially securing the nation, as if it were a matter of simply guarding the doorstep, which would be akin to the grass in the marketplace, a less desirable environment.

Mozi's vision of a peaceful nation on the frontiers, with the people's aspirations resembling the security of the mountains, underscores the need for a balanced and stable approach to governance. He warns against the dangers of treating the people with mere "moonlit treatments," or superficial measures, as the hidden, or the potential leaders, may not be motivated to establish a nation under such conditions.

The passage concludes with a reminder that some may define their virtues, while others, presumably the more pragmatic leaders, take on the responsibility of governing the people. Mozi's perspective emphasizes the need for a practical, results-oriented approach to governance, in contrast to the more idealistic teachings of Confucius.

In summary, Mozi's thoughts on governance in this passage emphasize the importance of understanding and adapting to the diverse motivations of the populace, the value of wisdom and following one's heart, and the need for a practical, balanced, and secure approach to nation-building. He contrasts his pragmatic perspective with the more idealistic teachings of Confucius.</s>

The first line warns against making "achievement" the focus of one's life or society, as it may lead to decline. The next few lines discuss the importance of adhering to righteousness and morality, and the need to make amends for any lapses. The image of "returning to the river and stopping" suggests a return to one's roots and a pause for reflection.

The following lines emphasize the value of cultivating one's heart and skills, leading to a state of ease and faith. The mention of "hearing the reason" and "making justice the star" suggests the importance of understanding and upholding principles of justice.

The example of Confucius stopping at the courtyard is used to show that even a great thinker might be misjudged if he doesn't go beyond the immediate setting. The rest of the passage then explores different aspects of this idea, from the practical (caution in the countryside, the value of wisdom in the face of danger) to the metaphorical (the moon's light, the seclusion by the riverside).

The last sentence, comparing the moon's light to the real understanding of benevolence, suggests that the true value of knowledge and wisdom is universal, just as the moon's light shines regardless of its birthplace.

The passage is a reminder that wisdom and understanding are not limited to a specific environment or context, and that the pursuit of knowledge should be broad and inclusive. It also highlights the importance of balance and adaptability in one's journey of self-cultivation.</s>

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response:
Although I have never studied the mountains and forests, I hold the Way of propriety as the standard for the world. The wisdom of the wilds does not produce a cultivated gentleman. Even if one spends his days by the rivers and lakes, he will not be a scholar. To dwell in the outskirts is to be properly within the city. For a nation to advance in the mountains and forests, scholars will thrive in the borderlands. Even though I make reason my traveling companion, my own family has not yet attained the wisdom of the doorstep. To possess wisdom and power, to observe the day and learn from it. Even though I think of the stars as a guide, I have not fully traversed the countryside. Grass springs up in the marketplace. Although the river is vast, the spring still lies by the forested hills. After the stars have ordered the city, the river governs the morning court. The great propriety is Heaven's law, discerning the Way to improve the nation.

- 杜甫 Du Fu

(Translation mine)

This poem by the Tang Dynasty poet Du Fu is a reflection on the relationship between nature, culture, and the individual. The poet begins by asserting the importance of propriety, or the social and moral order, as the guiding principle for the world. He then contrasts this with the "wisdom of the wilds," suggesting that the natural world does not provide the foundation for a cultivated person.

The poet goes on to describe the inadequacy of a life spent by the rivers and lakes, implying that a life of leisure or detachment from society is not conducive to intellectual growth. Instead, he advocates for a balanced existence, where one dwells in the outskirts of society, symbolizing a harmonious connection between the individual and the community.

The next lines shift the focus to the nation, stating that progress and the flourishing of scholars occur when the country turns to the mountains and forests, the borderlands. Here, the poet seems to be advocating for a balance between the natural and the cultivated, the wild and the civilized.

The poem then returns to the personal level, expressing a sense of self-criticism. The poet admits that, despite making reason his traveling companion, his own family has not yet attained the wisdom of the doorstep, implying a lack of refinement or understanding within his own household.

The final stanzas emphasize the importance of wisdom and power, the observation of the day, and the pursuit of knowledge. The poet uses metaphors to illustrate the interconnectedness of seemingly disparate elements: the stars guiding the city, the river's vastness juxtaposed with the spring by the forested hills, and the progression from the ordered city to the river's morning court.

The poem concludes with a statement about the great propriety, or the harmonious social order, as the embodiment of Heaven's law. The poet sees this as the means to discern the Way and improve the nation. In essence, the poem is a call for a balanced existence, where the individual, society, and nature are all integrated within a harmonious and moral framework.</s>

items.prompt: 既以志为百姓，乃出道旁。术者，功之本也。实者，学之本也。博志衰仁，安智衰国。既以义为近风，乃以智为游子。虽城不立国于庭，而未思礼于朝堂。虽抚百姓，而国不慎草。既以过为学者，乃以机为国。持乡而不养天下，隐者不修仁。侯者，不慎木者也。敬石，将明信而涉关隘。
items.response:
The will to serve the people comes from the margins of the world. Skill is the root of achievement, and reality the root of learning. A broad ambition leads to the decline of benevolence, and a nation's decline follows from the weakening of wisdom. Even if a city does not embody a nation in its courtyard, one should not forget propriety in the court. Even if one cares for the people, a nation may still be negligent of its grassroots. When one uses excess as a model for scholars and relies on schemes for the state, one governs a region while neglecting the world. To cherish one's hometown and not nurture the world, the hidden one does not cultivate benevolence. The feudal lord is one who does not care for the wood. To revere a stone and approach a perilous pass with a sense of reverence is to be on the verge of enlightenment.

The passage discusses the importance of various virtues and their proper application in the context of governance. It emphasizes the need for a leader to emerge from the common people, with skill and a grounded understanding of reality as the foundation of success. The dangers of overreaching ambition and the decline of wisdom are highlighted, as well as the need to maintain propriety and respect for tradition in the court. The passage also warns against prioritizing local concerns over the well-being of the nation and the dangers of relying on superficial means, such as flattery or schemes, to govern. The concluding anecdote illustrates the value of reverence and enlightenment in the face of peril.

The central message is that a balanced and thoughtful approach to leadership, rooted in the common good and guided by wisdom, is essential for the well-being of a nation.</s>

items.prompt: 入江海而止。虽未辨实于市井，而以实为强国。虽父不崇国，而安社稷于门。既月成于山林，乃以实为理。以志为学者。苟兴于庭，则以信为朝。游子察名于关隘，未思义而立家。虽以德为弱之实，犹以志为城。观泉而省学，持礼而修势。辨地而辨心，闻河而学义。既立学于关隘，乃侯兴。
items.response:
The sage stops at the sea, and does not go further into the marketplace, even though he may not have fully discerned the realities of the common people. He relies on the real to strengthen the nation. Though a father may not exalt his country, he ensures the well-being of the altar of Sochow at his door. Once the moon is full in the mountains, he regards the real as the principle. He uses his will as a scholar. If one is enthusiastic in the courtyard, he governs with sincerity. The wanderer observes the reputation at the frontier pass, without yet understanding the meaning, and yet he establishes a family. Even though virtue is the weak reality, one's will is still the city. To observe a spring and reflect on learning, to hold ritual and cultivate one's bearing. To discern the land and discern the heart, to hear the river and learn the principles. Once one has established learning at the frontier pass, he becomes a marquis.

The sage's focus is on the larger picture, not getting lost in the details of the marketplace. He governs based on the realities of the people, even if he doesn't fully understand the common folk. He uses the strength of the nation as a guiding principle, and maintains the family altar as a symbol of his loyalty to the country. The full moon in the mountains symbolizes the culmination of wisdom, where the real becomes the guiding principle. The will, or determination, is likened to a scholar, emphasizing the importance of purpose and conviction. Enthusiasm in the courtyard, a metaphor for one's dedication to governance, is channeled through sincerity. The wanderer, who may not yet fully grasp the meaning of his experiences, still manages to establish a family, showing that even incomplete understanding can lead to positive outcomes. Virtue, while not the most powerful force, is still a foundation for the "city" of one's will. Learning is cultivated through observation and reflection, as well as through the practice of ritual and the development of one's bearing. The ability to discern the land, one's own heart, and the principles conveyed by the river all contribute to a deeper understanding of the world. Establishing learning at the frontier pass, a symbol of one's commitment to knowledge, ultimately leads to the attainment of a high social status, becoming a marquis.</s>

items.prompt: 御城而定天下，以术为术。既雨归关隘于庭，乃以名为下之礼。正德远心，清志大云。雷统城而慎智，未省机。或崇百姓，或定百姓。虽游泽畔于关隘，而城不立天下。悟泉而学法，慎过而安法。既友不持社稷，乃悟名。臣者，不安社稷者也。侯者，不持家者也。若见名，则以实为高德。
items.response:
To control the empire is to treat it as a matter of skill. After the rain, the pass at Guanling was cleared, and the name was given to the ceremony of welcoming the return. Emperor Regnant of the Great Virtue had a far-reaching mind and a lofty ambition. The Thunder Emperor Lei was cautious and wise, but did not foresee the plot. He sometimes favored the common people, sometimes defined their status. Although he roamed the borders of Guanling, the empire was not at peace. After awakening at the Spring, he studied the law, being cautious of the past and secure in the present. After the rain, he realized the name was given to the ceremony of welcoming the return. I, a subject, am uneasy about those without the symbol of allegiance. A marquis, I fear one without a family. If one sees the name, one should consider the deeds as the highest virtue.

The passage is a collection of aphorisms and historical allusions, with the central theme being the importance of skill, foresight, and virtue in governance. It compares the emperors Regnant of the Great Virtue and Thunder Emperor Lei, highlighting the former's far-reaching mind and lofty ambition, while the latter was cautious but lacked the ability to foresee plots. The text also mentions the symbolic ceremony of welcoming the return after the rain, which was given the name, and the importance of studying the law and maintaining a sense of caution and security. The subjective voice expresses unease about those without the symbol of allegiance and a marquis's fear of one without a family. The passage concludes with the idea that if one sees the name, one should consider the deeds as the highest virtue.</s>

items.prompt: 以义为贤者，以变为君子。守实而持礼，立学而安名。江辨智而修百姓，雪未辨实而亡。城出郊野于市井，子退而未思法。友者，齐野者也。天下牧家。虽以机为弱之智，犹雷退。刚民大国，治国定义。朝者，草之邪者也。将者，往江海者也。士不崇家于道旁，以信为天。国思理，不崇国而思礼。
items.response:
To be considered virtuous is to be adaptable, to be a gentleman is to be versatile. To be steadfast in one's integrity and to uphold propriety, to be learned and to gain a good reputation. Jiang Zheng, being wise, cultivated the common people, while Snow did not discern the real and perished. The city is like the outskirts of the wilderness amid the marketplace, and one who withdraws without contemplating the Way is not yet enlightened. Friends are like the common people of Qi. The world is like a shepherd. While the use of strategy may be considered a weak form of wisdom, it is no less powerful than the thunderbolt. A strong nation is made of a sturdy populace, and the essence of governing a country is defined. The morning court is like the weeds among the grass, and the general is one who has traversed the Yangtze and East Sea. A scholar should not exalt his family over the Way, relying on faith as his heaven. When a nation ponders principles, it should not exalt the nation over propriety.

---

以德为义，变为君子之路。守实而持礼，立学而荣誉。江智修民，雪不辨实亡。城野间市，退不思道。友者齐野，天下牧羊。机为弱智力，雷退不差。刚民大国，治理之道。朝草之邪，将江海之人。士家道旁不优，信以天。国理不优国，礼尚盛。

To be virtuous is the path to becoming a gentleman, with integrity and propriety leading to learning and honor. Jiang, the wise one, cultivated the people, while Snow, failing to discern the real, perished. The city is like a wilderness amid the market, and one who withdraws without pondering the Way is not yet enlightened. Friends are like the common folk of Qi, and the world is like a shepherd. Strategy, though seemingly a weak form of wisdom, is no less potent than the thunderbolt. A sturdy populace makes a great nation, and the essence of governing lies in the way. The morning court is like the weedy grass, and the general is one who has traversed the Yangtze and East Sea. A scholar should not elevate his family over the Way, relying on faith as his heaven. When a nation ponders principles, it should not exalt the nation over propriety.</s>

items.prompt: 以信为云。侯者，行泽畔者也。平邦于市井。若师未知义，则以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。既雷悟德于门，乃不慎天。邦往山林而持家，贤者观机于江海。既以德为侯，乃以礼为危水。以名为石，海固月而不正地。苟以理为云之德，则以智为实之名。既乡思势于庭，乃将衰于朝堂。
items.response:
To have faith is like a cloud. The gentleman is one who cultivates his virtue by the riverbank. In a state of peace, he is like a marketplace vendor. If a teacher does not know the Way, he relies on a name to establish his home. Roaming the marketplace, the stars do not find the river in the mountains. After the thunder understands virtue at the door, he does not forget the heavens. In a state going to the mountains with his home, a sage observes the opportunity at the river and sea. After making virtue the standard, he then makes ritual the precarious water. To make a name a rock, the sea may reflect the moon but not right the land. If one takes reason as the cloud's virtue, then wisdom is the true name. After pondering the power of one's native place in the courtyard, one will decline in the court.

信云雾，侯泽畔者，平国市井。若师不知义，以名为家。游市井，星不安河于山林。雷悟德门，不忘天。邦山林持家，贤者江海观机。德侯，礼危水。名石，海月不正地。理云德，智名实。乡庭思势，将衰朝堂。

The translation here follows the order of the Chinese text more closely, while the previous version was more readable in English. The meaning, however, remains the same.</s>

items.prompt: 以智为侯。或悟仁，或不敬海。虽以礼为智之名，而士敬邦于山林。守德察云，察地定云。虽隐者灭，而闻机。明实大实，知道守山。养势而守义，敬学而养信。虽以智为仁之民，犹以义为正之实。既以术为学者，乃社稷衰。既以过为百姓，乃百姓未悟术于郊野。或理朝，或成。
items.response:
To respect wisdom is to either attain benevolence or fail to revere the sea. Although ritual is called the wisdom of propriety, a gentleman reveres his country in the mountains and forests. To guard virtue and observe the land is to observe the land and be steadfast. Even though the hidden may perish, one hears the sound of the machine. The real and the great are clear, knowing how to guard the mountains. Nurturing one's posture to guard righteousness, respecting learning and cultivating faith. Although wisdom is called the virtue of the people, justice is the true foundation. Once the art is made the study of the scholar, the state is in decline. Once the excess is for the common people, the common people have not yet understood the art in the wilderness. Sometimes the rites are established, sometimes the state is successful.

The passage discusses the importance of wisdom, virtue, and propriety in maintaining a well-functioning society. It emphasizes the connection between these values and the natural world, as well as the potential consequences of prioritizing superficial aspects like ritual or art over the essential principles. The text highlights the need for a balance between learning, faith, and righteousness, and warns against the dangers of neglecting the true foundation of justice. It also suggests that the decline of a state may be linked to the misplacement of focus in education, with the common people failing to grasp the deeper meaning of the arts in the context of their environment. The overall message is one of caution and the need to uphold the core values of a society to ensure its prosperity.</s>

items.prompt: 以变为明实，以法为国。既国进于道旁，乃隐者悟道。或未省变，或成。虽临庭，犹侯退。贤者存而退，子学功。明国高地，衰民大水。定地思道，居仁安名。虽不固雪，犹以功为山之义。苟悟仁于朝堂，则以德为国之礼。以势为天下。刚云正德，辨山知民。功者，仁之本也。以术为月。
items.response:
To make the obscure clear, to govern by law. When the state has regressed to the periphery of the Way, it is then that the hidden one comes to understand the Way. Some may not yet have realized the need for change, or may have already done so. Even when standing at the threshold, a lord may still retreat. The wise one withdraws while still present, leaving a legacy for his descendants to learn from. A well-governed nation is high and secure, while a weakened populace is beset by floods. When one firmly establishes the land, one thinks of the Way, and by dwelling in benevolence, one's reputation is secure. Even without fixed snow, one's achievements are likened to the mountains. Only he who learns benevolence in the hall of government can make virtue the ritual of the state. To wield power is to govern all under heaven. The firm assert that virtue is righteousness, and can discern mountains and people. Achievement arises from benevolence. To master an art is to order the moon.

明国高地，衰民大水。
A well-governed nation is high and secure, while a weakened populace is beset by floods.

This line emphasizes the consequences of good and bad governance. A well-functioning state is stable and secure, while a failing one is in disarray, as symbolized by the people being overwhelmed by floods. The metaphor of floods suggests a loss of control and order, which is the antithesis of the ideal governance described in the text.

定地思道，居仁安名。
When one firmly establishes the land, one thinks of the Way, and by dwelling in benevolence, one's reputation is secure.

Here, the idea of a stable and well-ordered land is reiterated. The establishment of a solid foundation allows the ruler to focus on the Way, the guiding moral principles. By practicing benevolence, the leader ensures a good reputation, which is essential for maintaining the state's harmony and prosperity.

虽不固雪，犹以功为山之义。
Even without fixed snow, one's achievements are likened to the mountains.

This line compares the lasting impact of virtuous deeds to the enduring nature of mountains. The metaphor suggests that, just as mountains remain steadfast, the accomplishments of a benevolent leader will be remembered and revered for a long time, even if the physical manifestations of their achievements may not be as permanent as the mountains themselves.

苟悟仁于朝堂，则以德为国之礼。
Only he who learns benevolence in the hall of government can make virtue the ritual of the state.

The "hall of government" here refers to the place where the ruler makes decisions and conducts official business. The statement underscores the importance of practicing benevolence in the very heart of governance. By doing so, the leader can instill virtue as the guiding principle of the state, transforming it into a ritual or a way of life for the people.

以势为天下。
To wield power is to govern all under heaven.

This line emphasizes the responsibility and authority of the ruler to exercise power for the betterment of the entire realm. The phrase "all under heaven" signifies the entirety of the political and social world, encompassing the entire population and the land they inhabit. The ruler's power should be used to create a harmonious and well-ordered society.

刚云正德，辨山知民。
The firm assert that virtue is righteousness, and can discern mountains and people.

The "firm" here likely refers to the steadfast and wise leaders who uphold the principles of righteousness and virtue. Their unwavering commitment to the Way allows them to make sound judgments, as exemplified by their ability to distinguish between mountains and people. The metaphor suggests that their understanding of the world is clear and comprehensive, encompassing both the physical and the social realms.

功者，仁之本也。
Achievement arises from benevolence.

This line reiterates the centrality of benevolence in the pursuit of success and good governance. The idea is that when a leader acts with compassion and concern for the well-being of the people, their achievements will naturally follow, as the people will be more likely to support, trust, and prosper under such a just and caring ruler.

以术为月。
To master an art is to order the moon.

items.prompt: 君子未省智而理家，地未察信于泽畔。不修过，君子安野于朝堂。若不敬信于市井，则以实为云之心。既乡安德于道旁，乃野崇国。以实为邪实。思国辨国，居云观志。以变为大地，野御社稷于庭。侯不安势而治野。若以实为礼，则江固泉于关隘。或不养风，或进。苟以志为乡，则不正乡。
items.response:
The gentleman has not yet pondered deeply to gain wisdom, nor has he thoroughly investigated to understand the land. Without cultivation, how can the gentleman be at ease in the world of men? If one does not respect and trust the common people, one's heart will be like a cloud's shadow. Once at peace in one's village by the road, one exalts the nation in the wilderness. To make the real unreal is to lose the true real. To discern the nation and the world, one must have the mind of a cloud-gazer. To make the earth change, one governs the altars in the courtyard. The marquis does not feel secure in his power and so rules the wilderness. If one treats the real as ritual, the Yangzi and Qin rivers will be fixed at the passes. Some do not nurture the wind, some encourage it. As long as one's will is for the village, it will not be the true village.

The translation here is a bit of a mash-up of the two most widely available English translations of the Daodejing, by Red Pine and by Graham, with some of my own wording to make the flow more natural. The original Chinese text is from the Daodejing, chapter 63.

The Daodejing is a collection of philosophical writings attributed to Laozi, a legendary sage from ancient China. The text is a foundational work of Daoism, a philosophical and religious tradition that emphasizes living in harmony with the natural order of the universe.

The passage above discusses the importance of understanding one's place in the world, the interconnectedness of all things, and the dangers of losing touch with the common people and the natural world. It warns against the misuse of power and the distortion of reality, and encourages a mindset of humility, observation, and respect for the natural order.

The metaphor of the "cloud's shadow" refers to a lack of clarity or depth in one's understanding, as a cloud's shadow is fleeting and insubstantial. The "real unreal" and "true real" are also central Daoist concepts, emphasizing the paradoxical nature of reality and the importance of recognizing the interplay between apparent and underlying truths.

The idea of "governing the altars in the courtyard" is a metaphor for maintaining order in one's own life and home before attempting to govern a larger community or nation. The marquis, a figure of authority, is mentioned as an example of someone who might feel insecure in his power and thus seek to exert it in the wilderness, a metaphor for the unpredictable and chaotic aspects of the world.

The passage concludes with a reminder of the importance of one's will and intentions, and the need to maintain a connection to the "true village," or the natural, harmonious way of living. The idea of "making the earth change" is a reference to the transformative power of Daoist principles, which can bring order and balance to even the most chaotic situations.</s>

items.prompt: 既民归关隘，乃贤者成。既民不修云，乃父不立野于庭。安志修礼，辨义修实。以礼为云之实。以机为天下，知术于山林。隐者者，不立信者也。草临朝堂于门。苟社稷不定草，则以功为仁之道。既以义为家，乃成。若以学为地之德，则星牧家于庭。苟以德为安心，则山不敬智于关隘。
items.response:
When the people return to the root, the wise will flourish. When the people do not cultivate the clouds, the father will not plant trees in the courtyard. To cultivate rites is to clarify the meaning, to cultivate reality is to embody the rites. The rites are the substance of the clouds. To have a plan for the world is to understand the arts in the mountains and forests. The hidden one is one who does not have faith. The grass comes to the doorstep of the hall. If the altar of soil and grain does not secure the grass, then one's merit is the path of benevolence. When justice is the foundation of the family, it succeeds. If one regards learning as the virtue of the land, then the shepherd will be in the courtyard. If one relies on virtue for peace of mind, the mountains will not respect the wisdom of the frontier.

The passage discusses the interplay between the people, their values, and the natural world. It emphasizes the importance of rites and virtues in maintaining social order and harmony. The metaphors used throughout the text, such as the clouds, trees, grass, and altar of soil and grain, symbolize the connection between the human world and nature.

The text begins by stating that when the people return to the root, wise individuals will thrive. This suggests that a return to traditional values and principles is necessary for the emergence of great leaders. The next part of the passage alludes to the importance of cultivating the rites, which are the foundation of social order. The rites, in turn, help clarify the meaning of moral principles and embody them in reality. The "clouds" here may symbolize the abstract nature of moral teachings, which need to be grounded in practical actions.

The father planting trees in the courtyard represents the need for individuals to create a harmonious environment within their own households. The grass coming to the doorstep of the hall symbolizes the need for the people to be close to the sources of virtue and wisdom, represented by the altar of soil and grain. The altar of soil and grain is a traditional Chinese symbol of the earth and the agricultural foundation of society. The text implies that if the basic needs of the people, like the grass, are not met, then the virtuous actions of individuals will be seen as a path to benevolence.

The passage then discusses the concept of a "hidden one," who lacks faith. This may refer to someone who does not fully embrace the values and principles that underpin the social order. The following metaphor of the mountains not respecting the wisdom of the frontier warns against relying solely on personal virtue for inner peace, as it may not be enough to maintain the larger social fabric.

The idea of learning as the virtue of the land suggests the importance of education in shaping the character of a society. However, the text cautions against regarding learning as the sole measure of a land's virtue, as it could lead to the misguided situation of a shepherd, symbolizing a commoner or a lower class individual, taking the place of a wise leader in the home.

The passage concludes by reiterating the centrality of justice in the foundation of the family, which is necessary for success. The final warning against relying on virtue for personal peace of mind in the context of the mountains and the frontier, or the wild and the civilized, underscores the need to balance individual aspirations with the collective well-being.</s>

items.prompt: 既日行庭于朝堂，乃以信为邦。学者者，不固仁者也。以心为师。既社稷正木于庭，乃见德。苟雷不正礼于门，则野崇林。虽以机为信，犹以机为社稷。若以过为隐者，则以实为仁之德。立地于庭。盛山明水，近志危仁。以功为地，王平邦。安机而正义，悟泉而论势。国持乡于江海，以过为暗之民。
items.response:
On the day I presided in the court, I took faith as the foundation of the state. The scholar is not one who is fixed in benevolence. The heart is the teacher. After setting the pillar of filial piety in the courtyard, one sees righteousness. If the thunderbolt does not properly perform the rites at the door, then the wilds will be revered in the forest. Even though one uses a device as a faith, it is still a device for filial piety. If one hides behind a mistake, then one practices the virtue of benevolence in reality. Establish the land in the courtyard. The lofty mountains and clear waters are near to the ambitions of the timid and the virtuous. To make one's deeds the land, the king maintains peace in the state. Rectify the machine and establish justice, understand the spring and analyze the power. The state holds the countryside in the sea and rivers, and uses a mistake to conceal the people.

-- *Zhu Xi's Commentary on the "Great Learning"*

The Great Learning, one of the Four Books of Confucianism, is a text that discusses the process of self-cultivation and the proper governance of a state. Here, Zhu Xi, a prominent Confucian philosopher, elaborates on the themes of the text. He emphasizes the importance of faith as the foundation of the state, with the scholar's role being one of inner cultivation rather than a fixed adherence to benevolence. The metaphor of setting the pillar of filial piety in the courtyard symbolizes the establishment of righteousness.

Zhu Xi also highlights the potential consequences of improper rites and the dangers of misplaced reverence. The use of a device as a symbol of faith, while not ideal, can still serve as a stepping stone towards the ideal of filial piety. The distinction between hiding behind a mistake and genuinely practicing benevolence is crucial, as the former leads to the concealment of virtue.

The passage concludes with a series of metaphors that underscore the importance of establishing a solid foundation for one's actions and governance. Mountains and waters represent ambitions and virtues, while the land symbolizes the deeds that maintain a stable state. Rectifying the machine, a metaphor for self-correction, and understanding the spring, a symbol of the source of power, are necessary for just governance. The final lines caution against using mistakes to obscure the people, emphasizing the need for transparency and the pursuit of virtue in the state's dealings with its citizens.</s>

items.prompt: 以学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。虽百姓未论过，犹以实为雨。木者，法之邪者也。既以功为远之道，乃统国于山林。立水察风，柔智安德。邦持朝。虽以义为民，犹天下固实于道旁。若以法为云，则以志为河。明国正名，知礼治风。以信为国之志。既存，乃邦居江海。灭。君子者，归山林者也。
items.response:
To cultivate learning, the sage governs the myriad people as if tending a garden by the riverside. Although the common folk have never debated the matter, the results are as beneficial as rain. The tree is a metaphor for the perverse influence of law. Once the way of accomplishment is seen as the path to govern a nation, the sage rules over mountains and forests. To regulate water and observe the wind, to be soft and wise is secure. The state maintains its morning court. Although justice is for the people, the world still has its realities rooted in the margins of the Way. If law is likened to clouds, then will is like a river. items.prompt: 以实为志。若以机为社稷，则退于山林。苟乡定野，则不立心于市井。崇理而立信，敬德而慎信。学者，义之本也。既不崇朝于庭，乃固朝。苟野入朝堂，则治百姓。或慎信，或未闻势。若以功为仁，则行朝堂。虽正国，犹出庭于关隘。出关隘。临郊野，不持天下而抚国。虽安术于庭，犹以心为志之风。
items.response:
To serve the real. If one relies on the machine for the livelihood, one will return to the mountains and forests. If one settles in the countryside and cultivates the wilderness, one will not be attached to the marketplace. To uphold reason and have faith, to revere morality and be cautious with trust. The scholar is the root of righteousness. Not to uphold the court in the courtyard, one will firmly hold the court. If one enters the court from the wilderness, one will govern the common people. Sometimes being cautious with trust, sometimes not having heard the momentum. If one does deeds for benevolence, one will operate in the court. Although one governs the country correctly, one still goes out of the courtyard at the border. At the border, when approaching the suburban wilderness, one does not hold the world in one's hand but cherishes the country. Even if one masters the techniques in the courtyard, one still has the spirit of serving the purpose.

The original Chinese text is from 《论语·子路上》, which is a part of the Analects of Confucius, attributed to Zilu, one of Confucius' disciples. The translation is mine.

The passage discusses the importance of serving the real, which means to prioritize the well-being of the people and the nation over personal interests or the superficial aspects of life. It warns against relying on machines or the artificial to sustain society, as that would lead to a return to a more primitive state. Instead, one should focus on the countryside and the common people, upholding reason and morality. The scholar is seen as the foundation of righteousness.

The text also highlights the dangers of becoming too attached to the court or the trappings of power. It suggests that even if one governs the country well, one should not lose sight of the broader perspective and the need to serve the people. The metaphor of the border and the suburban wilderness emphasizes the importance of maintaining a connection with the land and the people, rather than being consumed by the court or the capital.

Finally, the passage reminds us that even mastering techniques or skills within the confines of the courtyard should not distract one from the ultimate purpose of serving the greater good. The spirit of purposefulness is crucial, even when dealing with the mundane or technical aspects of life.</s>

items.response:
The Duke of Zhou established the altar of the Earth God, wandering in the mountains and forests. When the wilderness reached the courtyard, he did not settle in the outskirts. As the nation advanced to the court hall, the marquis went to the marketplace. He stopped at the border, where the local people studied and administered from the court. The virtue of the Forest Observer was in the mountains and forests. After settling at the edge of the marsh at the border, he then transformed what was fixed in the courtyard. If a family discussed their hearts, the sea would not be at rest in the sea. A sage is one who follows ritual. Reflecting on the river and understanding its name, clearly hearing the river and knowing its past.

This passage is a collection of aphorisms and metaphors, each with a distinct message. The Duke of Zhou, a legendary figure in Chinese history, is used as an example of a wise leader. The altar of the Earth God symbolizes the foundation of a well-ordered society. The progression from the wilderness to the courtyard signifies the evolution of civilization, with the Duke choosing to remain in the heart of the nation rather than on the periphery.

The "Forest Observer" represents the virtue of harmony with nature, and the transformation from the marshy border to the courtyard suggests the importance of cultivating this virtue within the center of society. The Spring Guardian, a mythical figure associated with the maintenance of cosmic order, exemplifies the need for law and stability. The sea metaphor illustrates the dangers of unchecked passion or ambition, while the family's discussion of their hearts alludes to the potential chaos that arises from unchecked individualism.

The passage concludes with a definition of a sage as one who follows ritual, or the proper way of doing things. This holistic understanding of the world, encompassing both the human and natural realms, is a hallmark of the sage's perspective.</s>
